does: This paper introduces VCap, a novel reward mechanism for reinforcement learning in visual captioning that uses a Witness-Adjudicator design pairing reference captions with visual signals to verify factual consistency. The core contribution is a reward signal grounded in hypergeometric distribution principles that provides fine-grained feedback on caption quality by explicitly checking whether generated captions align with both reference text and visual content. The method enables weak-to-strong generalization, allowing models to learn from imperfect reference captions. Experiments demonstrate that an 8B multimodal language model trained with VCap achieves strong performance improvements over existing open-source and closed-source baselines, indicating the reward design effectively drives models toward better precision and coverage in visual captioning tasks.
connects: This work builds on the established paradigm of using reinforcement learning to improve multimodal language models for captioning, extending recent efforts to move beyond supervised fine-tuning toward RL-driven optimization. It addresses limitations in existing reward designs for captioning that fail to provide reliable signals for factual verification, drawing on broader challenges in aligning vision-language models with grounded facts. The approach connects to classical work on hallucination mitigation in vision-language understanding and builds on the theoretical foundation of reward design in RL, while engaging with the weak-to-strong generalization framework that has shown promise in other domains. The paper responds to the challenge of training from imperfect data, a persistent problem in scaling multimodal systems.
enables: VCap's reward mechanism opens pathways for improving the reliability and factual grounding of visual captioning systems at scale, particularly enabling better leveraging of unlabeled or noisy reference data for RL training. The hypergeometric precision framework could extend beyond captioning to other vision-language tasks requiring fine-grained factual verification, such as visual question answering or image-text matching. The weak-to-strong generalization capability suggests potential applications in data-efficient training regimes and could facilitate scaling captioning systems with limited high-quality supervision. Further work could explore integrating this reward design with newer architectures, extending verification mechanisms to video understanding, or applying similar factual consistency checks across broader multimodal reasoning tasks where hallucination and omission remain open problems.
